source.title: Kentucky aviation laws, rules and regulations. 1946
source.author: Kentucky.

pages.text:
The General Assembly at its regular 1944 session authorized the Kentucky Aeronautics Commission to supervise, control and regulate intrastate transportation of freight and passengers by aircraft for hire. It provided that before any person shall engage in the public transportation by aircraft of persons or property in intrastate commerce, it must first obtain a Certificate of Convenience and Necessity from the Commission, except those persons operating on the basis of their prior operations shall be granted a Certificate under the grandfather clause of the Act, which Certificate shall be effective from the effective date of the Act. Applications for a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity were required to be made in writing to the Commission accompanied by the filing fee of $25, proof of insurance, and such other things as required by the Commission. The Commission was authorized to make enough charges annually for the use of the air routes in Kentucky as necessary to pay the expenses of the Commission for services rendered in carrying out their duties in regulating air traffic. However, these charges were limited so as not to exceed two per cent of the gross receipts of common carriers operating under the Act. Before the Commission was authorized to issue a Certificate of Convenience and Necessity, it was required to hold a hearing to determine the public need and necessity for the routes applied for. All interested persons were required to be given thirty days notice of the hearing so they could participate therein. The Commission was required to set up and establish rules and regulations governing the conduct and procedure of such hearings.

The Legislature provided that the applicant or holder of a Certificate might appeal to the Circuit Court of Franklin County from orders of the Commission, and the further right to appeal from the Franklin Circuit Court to the Court of Appeals of Kentucky. All common carriers subject to the Act were required to file with the Commission, and keep open for inspection at their offices and terminals, schedules showing all rates, fares and charges for the transportation of passengers and property. The Commission was invested with authority to supervise, regulate and fix, alter and determine just, fair, reasonable and sufficient rates, fares, charges and classifications. The Legislature also gave permission for private owners to apply to the Kentucky Aeronautics Commission to come within its jurisdiction. If upon inspection of the airport, it was found to conform to the standards of public airports, the application was required to be approved and the airport was deemed to be established and within the jurisdiction of the Commission, the same as public airports. After such an airport had been approved the owner then was permitted to invoke the aid of the Kentucky Aeronautics Commission in condemning property adjoining the airport needed for airport purposes. By submitting to the jurisdiction of the Kentucky Aeronautics Commission, the private airport owner was then permitted to extend and improve any runways of the airport over and across any county road or pass-way not designated by the Kentucky State Highway Department as a part of the primary road system of this Commonwealth. The Legislature also authorized the Department of Highway to furnish men, machinery, material, and equipment for the maintenance of runways only of any and all airports established by cities, counties, or cities and counties jointly, that have paved runways and which were approved by the CAA, except if the airport was under lease to the Federal Government or to a private corporation, in which event the Highway Department was then without authority to render this assistance.

The authority of each county containing a city of the first class was increased so as to constitute the City and County Air Board, a body politic and corporate. Its authority was extended to permit the Air Board to provide as many airports as were needed to care for the transportation of passengers, property, express and mail, and for the use of the Federal Government.

At the extraordinary session of the General Assembly which began on April 23, 1945, legislative authority was granted to any city or county, or city and county
